text.fullText: TO: ​ ​ United States Environmental Protection Agency FROM: ​ The Alaska Center and Cook Inletkeeper DATE: ​ February 17, 2026 RE: ​ ​ Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 The Alaska Center is a nonprofit organization working to engage, empower, and elect Alaskans to stand up for our clean air and water, healthy communities, and a strong democracy. Cook Inletkeeper is a community-based nonprofit dedicated to protecting Alaska’s Cook Inlet watershed and the life it sustains. On behalf of our members and supporters, we submit the following comments. We strongly oppose the Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA) proposed update to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act. This change limits the ability of states and tribal governments to review projects and possibly block them if they threaten waterways, wetlands, and drinking water. Federal, state, and tribal cooperation is fundamental to ensuring the public’s interests are represented and reflected in future developments — both in Alaska, and across the United States. Weakening Section 401 only serves to undermine important water protections. Overview Over the course of the last year Alaska has received disproportionate attention from the Trump Administration around a single topic — resource extraction.1 The current Administration has pushed its agenda onto Alaska’s land, waters, and communities with little input or regard for public opinion. Proposed activities, from an increase in mining to drilling for oil and deforestation, all have significant implications for Alaska’s water supplies. The timing of this proposal from the EPA makes it clear that the federal government is hopeful that easing safeguards around clean water will allow industries to build faster and with fewer regulations. In practice, Alaska’s natural resources are inherently unique, and deserve due consideration whenever they are subject to federal policies made in Washington, D.C. For example, Alaska is home to more than 40 percent of the entire nation's surface water resources.2 The state is 1 https://www.whitehouse.gov/presidential-actions/2025/01/unleashing-alaskas-extraordinary-resource-potential/ 2 https://dnr.alaska.gov/mlw/water/#:~:text=Alaska%20has%20more%20than%2040,%25%20surface%20water% 2C%2018%25%20groundwater 1 home to three million lakes, over 12,000 rivers, thousands of streams and creeks, and approximately 100,000 glaciers, according to the State Department of Natural Resources. These figures put the scale and potential impact to Alaska from the EPA’s proposal into focus. According to Alaska’s State Constitution, water is a public resource in Alaska intended to deliver maximum benefits to the public.3 These benefits are numerous in scope, as water drives much of Alaska’s state economy. In addition to providing 98 percent of the country’s salmon supply, local waters drive our recreational industry.4 Moreover, clean water is a backbone to our state’s subsistence resources and animal life. For these reasons, among others, Alaskans have long understood that water bodies require protection from overuse and contamination. Local Expertise Alaska is no stranger to the potential fallout of pollution and contamination driven by extractive industries. These experiences have proven that small missteps can have far-ranging geographic consequences, impacting Alaskan’s health, and the well-being of our environment. For example, fallout from the Red Dog Mine in Alaska, a massive zinc-lead operation, shows the serious consequences of contamination from mining activity. In 2016, the mine released 756 million pounds of toxic chemicals into the environment.5 That same year, according to the EPA’s Toxic Releases Inventory (TRI), the mine released more toxic waste than any other facility in the U.S. The town of Kotzeube, despite being 80 miles away, was declared the most industrially polluted town in the country as streams and rivers mixed with debris.6 Contaminants released from the mine included a number of materials harmful to human health. Runoff from these types of contaminates is easily transported through waterways and evaporation. Not surprisingly, this spike in waste was linked with poor water quality in the area. This raised concerns among many in the area dependent on subsistence resources which draw from this water supply, including both plant and animal life.7 Alaskans have also seen these hazards play out much closer to population centers. In 2009, it was reported that a former North Pole Refinery, located outside of Fairbanks, caused 3 https://law.justia.com/constitution/alaska/constitution-8.html 4 https://www.alaskaseafood.org/news/for-release-alaska-wild-salmon-day-honors-our-nations-favorite-fish-and-t he-people-who-provide-it/ 5 https://www.nationalgeographic.com/science/article/most-toxic-town-us-kotzebue-alaska-red-dog-mine 6 https://theworld.org/stories/2018/06/04/most-toxic-town-america 7 https://www.sciencealert.com/most-toxic-town-in-the-united-states-epa-chemicals-pollution-kotzebue-alaska 2 extensive groundwater contamination with sulfolane and PFAS. These substances migrated into local residential water supplies. A court later found poor maintenance and spills from the refinery responsible for this fallout.8 Residents were forced to find alternative water sources once the discovery was made. While we hope these experiences, and similar instances of water pollution, will never be repeated, it is in the public’s best interest to prevent similar happenings. The lessons learned from past incidents can help keep history from repeating itself - yet these lessons largely lie with locals. Removing state and tribal expertise effectively removes important knowledge from smart decision-making processes. It is important to understand that the Trump Administration's mineral and resource agenda would spike construction and extractive activities across Alaska. This volume alone increases the likelihood of incidents, and harm to both communities and local ecosystems. Health Impacts Approximately 31 Alaska Native communities lack piped, in-home running water in Alaska. These communities, which compromise roughly 1,700 homes, rely on hauling water from natural sources or community watering points.9 When we discuss safeguards to water in Alaska, we are discussing direct threats to drinking water in some cases. Alaska is also unique in the sense that water quality directly impacts principal food sources. As previously noted, salmon is a huge source of subsistence for Alaskan communities. These fish are a staple food for many larger mammals that are also hunted for subsistence and recreation. Water contamination is in essence, contamination of local food supplies. This dependence on natural resources should highlight the seriousness with which Alaskan communities approach clean water. Any direct impact to public health deserves the weigh in of anyone being put at risk - not just federal policymakers. Recently, the EPA announced it would no longer assign a cost value to human health and life when considering the cost-benefit calculations of air pollution activities.10 This raises a number of questions around how the federal government will evaluate projects, and what level of risk we can expect from future federal proposals. 8 https://alaskabeacon.com/briefs/alaska-supreme-court-finds-former-north-pole-refinery-owner-liable-for-pollutio n/ 9 https://www.adn.com/sponsored-content/2020/09/15/in-rural-alaska-access-to-water-is-preventative-medicine/#: ~:text=SPONSORED:%20Water%20and%20sanitation%20projects,with%20water%20for%2015%20years 10 https://www.nytimes.com/2026/01/12/climate/trump-epa-air-pollution.html 3 It is particularly disheartening to see tribal participation in decision-making processes minimized at a time when the U.S. government acknowledges it is following short on its legal duties to tribal nations.11 This is in spite of the fact that Traditional Ecological Knowledge, or Indigenous management styles of land and water have been previously shown to improve ecosystems and help mitigate climate change.12 Removing tribal participation is a clear step in the wrong direction. Adaptation and Resilience Alaska and the rest of the Arctic are on the front lines of climate change, warming four times faster than the rest of the planet.13 What this means, in a practical sense, is that Alaska's landscape and resources are changing at a rapid rate. Permafrost melt and changing temperatures are literally shifting the ground beneath our feet, and planning for new developments or construction requires advanced knowledge and foresight. Just this year, the Denali Commission has proposed adding five new climate-change driven threats to Alaska’s outlook. These include landslides, tsunamis, wildfires, earthquakes, and volcanoes.14 Adapting to these new challenges is essential, and both tribal and state bodies should be able to communicate their on-the-ground expertise as environmental norms continue to change. These new threats will undoubtedly impact projects that interact with our water resources, as well as the sensitivity of our waterways. In summary, The Alaska Center and Cook Inletkeeper strongly urges the EPA to not adopt the proposed revisions to Section 401. Upholding the participation of tribes and states in decision-making is vital to protecting public water sources, and maintaining clean water standards both now, and for future generations. We look forward to the opportunity to continue our engagement in this review process. Jennifer Hyde Satchel Pondolfino Federal Infrastructure Coordinator Clean Water Lead The Alaska Center Cook Inletkeeper jennifer@akcenter.org 11 https://grist.org/indigenous/us-government-says-it-is-falling-short-on-its-legal-duties-to-tribal-nations/ 12 Ibid. 13 https://www.nature.com/articles/s43247-022-00498-3 14 https://alaskabeacon.com/briefs/new-hazards-to-be-analyzed-in-alaskas-updated-statewide-threat-assessment / 4
